text: HRV-derived lower threshold (HRVTlow) shows a mean difference of only -0.6% bpm compared to ventilatory threshold (VTlow) and 0.02% bpm compared to lactate threshold (LTlow), making it a viable non-invasive alternative for determining the moderate-to-heavy intensity boundary.
specific_value: -0.6% bpm vs VTlow, 0.02% bpm vs LTlow

text: Individual-level agreement between HRVTlow and VTlow shows a bias of 1 bpm with limits of agreement of -10.9 to 12.8 bpm, meaning individual athletes could have HRV-derived thresholds up to ~12 bpm off from their true ventilatory threshold.

Individual-level agreement between HRVTlow and LTlow is wider than for VTlow, with bias of 2.7 bpm and limits of agreement of -20.4 to 25.8 bpm, suggesting HRV thresholds align better with ventilatory than lactate-based thresholds.

HRV-derived higher threshold (HRVThigh) shows a mean difference of 0.3% bpm compared to VThigh and 2.9% bpm compared to LThigh for determining the heavy-to-severe intensity boundary, though this requires further empirical validation.
specific_value: 0.3% bpm vs VThigh, 2.9% bpm vs LThigh

text: Three-zone intensity models (moderate, heavy, severe) delineated by a lower threshold (VT1/LT1) and higher threshold (VT2/LT2) can be approximated using HRV-derived thresholds during graded exercise testing, providing a non-invasive alternative to blood lactate or gas exchange analysis.
HRV-derived thresholds have been validated primarily in male athletes; evidence for female athletes is currently insufficient to draw conclusions about accuracy or applicability.
